citation.authors: Wonse Jo, Ruiqi Wang, Baijian Yang, Daniel Foti, Mo Rastgaar, Byung-Cheol Min
citation.title: Cognitive Load-Based Affective Workload Allocation for Multihuman Multirobot Teams

measured_constructs: Other / Not Listed (affective valence/arousal via the Self-Assessment Manikin, and physiological/behavioral cognitive-load signals used as an objective workload proxy)

study_type.justification: A formally powered, IRB-approved within-subject user study with 32 real participants (16 two-person teams), physical robot hardware, wearable biosensors, and statistical analysis (rmANOVA) of mission performance is the article's central validating evidence, making User Study Primary. The article also proposes and formalizes a reusable, application-agnostic controller (the DRL-based AWAC, its POMDP formulation, and the data-driven human performance model), explicitly framed as adaptable to other applications by tuning parameters, warranting System/Architecture Paper as Secondary.

summary.task: Two-operator teams performed a simulated CCTV monitoring task: six physical ground robots (SMARTmBOT) patrolled a corridor at ~300 mm/s while streaming video of separate rooms containing normal and 'abnormal' (skeleton) objects, and each operator had to click to flag abnormal objects across up to five camera-view windows assigned to them. Each team completed eight ~6-minute tasks (Task A-H) under different workload-allocation conditions (fixed, team-negotiated, subjective-CWL-driven, objective-CWL-driven, and combined, each with or without a consent step), totaling ~90 minutes.
summary.control: Operators had no direct control over the robots' motion at all — the robots followed a fixed, autonomously pure-pursuit-controlled patrol route regardless of operator action. Operators' only interactive controls were (a) clicking within a camera-view window to flag a detected abnormal object, and (b) in 'approval session' (AS) conditions, accepting or rejecting a workload (camera-count) reallocation proposed by the DRL-based AWAC controller, which itself decided reallocation based on operators' subjective (ISA self-report) and/or objective (physiological/behavioral, deep-learning-predicted) cognitive workload.
summary.participants: 32 participants (9 female, 23 male) forming 16 two-person teams, aged 18-34 (M=23.81, SD=4.17), compensated $15 plus a performance-based bonus; study reviewed and approved by Purdue University IRB (#IRB-2021-1813).
summary.results: Team mission-score comparisons (rmANOVA, α=0.10) found that AWAC conditions using objective (predicted) CWL (Task F) outperformed the fixed-workload baseline (Task A), and the combined subjective+objective condition (Task H) outperformed the subjective-only condition (Task D); among approval-session conditions, using both subjective and objective CWL together (Task G) achieved the best team performance. Subjective (SAM) valence scores were more positive under CWL-informed conditions, consistent with the Yerkes-Dodson framing underlying the model. No significant relationship was found between team-members' prior friendship and team performance. Post-interviews indicated most participants preferred dynamically changing workload over a fixed workload, and some found the subjective self-report (IS) and consent (AS) steps helpful.
summary.issues: Statistical significance was only reached at a relaxed α=0.10, not the conventional 0.05, attributed to the study's exploratory design and small sample (16 teams); the authors note p-values trended toward significance as more teams were added, suggesting the effect is real but underpowered. Some participants gave inaccurate subjective (ISA) self-reports, causing the AWAC to reallocate workload incorrectly in at least one condition (a sudden performance drop in Task D). Facial-feature extraction for the objective cognitive-workload model failed for participants wearing masks, reducing usable training data to 70% of the underlying dataset. The study tested only two operators and six robots due to space/equipment constraints, leaving scalability to larger teams unconfirmed. Only camera-view count was varied as a workload driver, even though the authors' own prior work found robot speed also significantly affects operator performance/workload, which was not manipulated here.
summary.recommendations: Conduct future studies with larger, more diverse participant samples to reach conventional statistical significance and improve generalizability. Test the AWAC's scalability with more human operators, more robots, and more complex team configurations. Incorporate additional workload-driving factors beyond camera-view count, such as robot speed. Tune the subjective/objective weighting parameters (currently fixed at 0.5/0.5) based on the objective model's actual prediction accuracy rather than a flat split. Retain a consent/approval step before workload changes, since consultation-based allocation was both preferred by participants and empirically effective.
summary.autonomous_capabilities: Each of the six ground robots autonomously executes a fixed patrol route between start and goal positions using a pure-pursuit control algorithm, with position tracked by an external Vicon motion-capture system, requiring no human path-planning or teleoperation input. Independently, the DRL-based AWAC controller autonomously computes and reallocates each operator's workload (number of assigned camera views) in real time, using a data-driven human performance model (grounded in the Yerkes-Dodson law) that converts subjective ISA self-report scores and a deep-learning-predicted objective cognitive-workload level (from a multimodal transformer model, Husformer, processing physiological and facial-behavioral biosignals) into an estimated operator performance signal that drives the reallocation policy (trained via Proximal Policy Optimization).

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: Normal and abnormal ('skeleton') objects are randomly placed across multiple separate rooms/camera views, each individually static once placed, rather than a single instance or a moving/spreading entity.

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: Operators must detect/identify abnormal objects among normal ones across streaming camera views, where each object's presence/identity is not known in advance and confirming it (a click) is the goal.

team_size.justification: Two human operators jointly supervise a fleet of six ground robots providing the camera streams.

team_composition.justification: All six SMARTmBOT platforms are identical and run the same patrol behavior.
The two participants in each team 'have identical roles and responsibilities in the team,' with symmetric task assignments.

interaction_model.justification: Each operator is assigned and simultaneously monitors up to five camera-view windows (robot feeds) at once.

At the team level, the pool of six camera views/robots is dynamically reallocated between the two operators by the shared AWAC system, so both operators and the robot pool are jointly connected through one shared control/allocation system.

interaction_directness.justification: Each robot's video feed is explicitly routed to and displayed for its assigned operator, and the operator's click/flag action is addressed to that specific feed.

communication_proximity.justification: Operators interact entirely through a screen-based GUI displaying streamed camera views; they are not physically co-located with the patrolling robots in any functionally relevant sense.

coordination_architecture.justification: Six robots are present, so multi-robot coordination conceptually applies, but the article describes each robot only as independently executing its own fixed pure-pursuit patrol route; no inter-robot coordination, leader, or negotiation mechanism among the robots themselves is described.

computational_characteristics.justification: Robot motion along the patrol route is generated by a named classical path-tracking algorithm, pure pursuit, repetitively driving each robot between fixed start and goal positions.

human_involvement_level.justification: Each robot's patrol function executes and delivers its video output regardless of operator action (operators cannot control or interrupt robot motion), but its output is monitored by the human, satisfying HOnL rather than HOffL.

The AWAC reallocation function's entire purpose depends on continuously ingesting the human operators' subjective and/or objective affective-state data; without that ongoing human input the function cannot determine how to reallocate workload as intended.

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: Operators exercise no navigational or robot-parameter control authority at any point in this study — their only actions are object-detection clicks and accepting/rejecting a proposed workload reallocation, neither of which sets any robot behavior or control parameter, so the control-granularity scale this attribute measures does not apply to their interface.

operational_domain.justification: The six SMARTmBOT platforms are ground robots patrolling an indoor corridor.

flags.note: The article never states whether or how the six patrol robots coordinate with one another (they appear to run independent, non-interacting fixed routes), even though multiple robots are present.

Operators in this study have no robot-control interface of any kind (only object-flagging clicks and workload-consent decisions), so LHCA — which measures the granularity of operator control input over a system — does not apply here despite an otherwise rich human-system interface.
